did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the YOLOv10 deep learning object detection model to detect and classify skin cancer (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis) and skin lesions using the ISIC 2017 and Skin Cancer Detection datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and evaluation of a YOLOv10-based machine learning model for skin cancer detection. It details specific methodologies (preprocessing, augmentation), datasets used (ISIC 2017, Skin Cancer Detection), and reports new quantitative results (precision, recall, mAP50). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICSIPA), it contains original research results rather than being a narrative or systematic review.

title: A Machine Learning-based Model for the Detection of Skin Cancer Using YOLOv10
journal: 2024 IEEE 8th International Conference on Signal and Image Processing Applications (ICSIPA)
brief_summary: This study develops a machine learning-based model using the YOLOv10 object detection architecture to detect and classify skin cancer lesions, specifically melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. The model was trained and evaluated on two datasets: the ISIC 2017 dataset and the SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2 dataset from Roboflow. Preprocessing and augmentation techniques, including resizing and rotation, were applied to enhance model performance before fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv10s weights. The evaluation approach utilized precision, recall, mAP50, and F1-score metrics to assess detection accuracy on both datasets. On the ISIC 2017 dataset, the model achieved a precision of 1, recall of 0.98, and mAP50 of 0.94. On the Skin Cancer Detection dataset, it achieved a precision of 1, recall of 0.95, mAP50 of 0.79, and an F1-score of 0.82, demonstrating effective lesion identification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a specific class in the ISIC 2017 dataset and as part of the 'malignant tumors' category in the Skin Cancer Detection dataset.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, malignant tumors, benign growths, healthy skin) with bounding boxes and class labels.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the YOLOv10 object detection model to identify and localize skin cancer lesions in dermoscopic images. The task involves predicting bounding boxes for specific lesion classes (multiclass detection) and classifying them into discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus vs. healthy skin). Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, mAP50, and F1-score, which are standard for object detection tasks involving classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: The 2017 ISIC challenge dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 2750 JPEG images (767x102 pixels). In this study, it was annotated in Roboflow and augmented to 546 images total.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv10s model with transfer learning from COCO pre-trained weights.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (precision, recall, mAP50).

datasets_used.name: SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2
datasets_used.description: A dataset from Roboflow Universe intended for skin detection. The original dataset contained 627 images. In this study, it was expanded to 1197 images via augmentation (resizing to 800x800, horizontal flips, brightness shifts).

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv10s model.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (precision, recall, mAP50, F1-score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using deterministic metrics (precision, recall, mAP50, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2017.notes: The model performed most accurately in identifying nevus and seborrheic keratosis lesions. It struggled to distinguish between melanoma and background clearly and exhibited confusion between melanoma and nevus lesions.

Skin Cancer Detection.notes: The paper refers to this dataset as 'Skin Detection Dataset' in the abstract and 'Skin Cancer Detection' in the results table. The model achieved high precision and recall.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from the COCO dataset

architecture_details.description: Advanced version of YOLO incorporating Non-Maximum Suppression-eliminated training and inference, and streamlined model formulation. Chosen for its efficiency and suitability for smaller datasets.

training_details.training_schedule: Transfer learning with fine-tuning on ISIC 2017 and SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2 datasets

classification_or_output_head.output: Bounding boxes and class labels for skin lesions

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 640x640 pixels (for ISIC 2017 dataset)
Resizing images to 800x800 pixels (for SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2 dataset)
Annotation in Roboflow for detection-based techniques

data_augmentation_techniques.details: 90° rotations (clockwise and counter-clockwise) applied to the ISIC 2017 dataset.
Hue adjustments between -15° and +15° applied to the ISIC 2017 dataset.
Saturation variations between -31% and +31% applied to the ISIC 2017 dataset.
Horizontal flips applied to the SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2 dataset.
Brightness shifts between -45% and +45% applied to the SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2 dataset.
Images resized to 640x640 pixels for ISIC 2017 and 800x800 pixels for SKIN_CANCER_DETECTION V2.
Auto-orientation technique applied during preprocessing for both datasets.

conclusions: The study concludes that the YOLOv10s model is a highly effective and efficient system for detecting skin lesions, achieving high performance metrics on both the ISIC 2017 and Skin Cancer Detection datasets.

On the ISIC 2017 dataset, the model achieved a precision of 1, recall of 0.98, and mAP50 of 0.94, demonstrating its capability to precisely identify the presence and locations of skin lesions in dermoscopic images.
On the Skin Cancer Detection dataset, the model achieved a precision of 1, recall of 0.95, mAP50 of 0.79, and an F1-score of 0.82.
The model performed most accurately in identifying nevus and seborrheic keratosis lesions.
The model exhibited limitations in distinguishing clearly between melanoma and the background, as well as confusion between melanoma and nevus lesions.

The use of preprocessing and augmentation techniques (such as resizing, rotation, hue/saturation adjustments, and brightness shifts) effectively enhanced the model's performance and training stability.
YOLOv10s was selected for its efficiency and suitability for smaller datasets, offering a balance of speed and accuracy compared to other state-of-the-art models.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset size (546 images for ISIC 2017 and 1197 for the Roboflow dataset) after augmentation, which raises concerns about the generalizability and robustness of the model compared to larger-scale benchmarks.

The model achieved a perfect precision of 1.0 on both datasets, which is unusually high for medical imaging tasks and may indicate overfitting, data leakage, or a lack of sufficient hard negative examples in the test set.

The ISIC 2017 dataset was significantly reduced in size for this study (from the original 2750 images to 546 augmented images), potentially discarding valuable data and limiting the diversity of the training set.

The model exhibited specific limitations in distinguishing melanoma from the background and showed confusion between melanoma and nevus lesions, indicating potential clinical risks for false negatives or misclassification of malignant cases.

The evaluation lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals), relying solely on deterministic metrics which does not account for variance or uncertainty in the model's performance.

The 'Skin Cancer Detection' dataset used is a smaller, non-standard collection from Roboflow rather than a widely recognized clinical benchmark (like HAM10000 or ISIC 2018/2019), limiting the ability to compare results directly with state-of-the-art literature.
The paper does not report external validation on an independent, unseen clinical dataset, which is critical for assessing real-world clinical relevance and deployment readiness.
